data.title: Kevin Warsh to be Fed's 'chairman rather than chief' - JPMorgan's David Kelly
data.description:
Kevin Warsh will need to prove himself as a consensus-builder rather than a dominant leader when he takes the helm at the Federal Reserve, JPMorgan Asset Management’s chief global strategist David Kelly said Wednesday.

“I think what it says is that Kevin Warsh is gonna be chairman rather than chief,” Kelly said in a CNBC interview, following a Fed meeting that saw four dissents from the committee’s decision.

Kelly noted that Warsh follows in the footsteps of giants, with only five Fed chairs serving since 1980, all of them intellectual giants. He praised outgoing Chair Jerome Powell as “an extraordinarily wise man, full of empathy and integrity” who “really brought the committee with him.”

The multiple dissents send a clear message to the incoming chair, Kelly said: “You’re really gonna have to talk with everybody in the committee. You’re gonna have to find consensus. You can’t just steamroll people with your own ideas here.”

Kelly also raised concerns about President Donald Trump’s expectations, noting that “the president thinks he has nominated a Fed chair who’s gonna cut rates” to do his bidding.

Powell said earlier he plans to remain on the central bank’s board of governors after his term as chair ends in mid-May," citing concerns about ongoing legal challenges to the institution, including the probe into the Fed’s building renovation project. He noted he would rather not influence the next chair and will keep "a low profile" as governor.

Questions remain about Warsh’s true policy views. Kelly pointed out that Warsh was hawkish during his previous Fed tenure but has recently expressed “almost the opposite set of views” in the lead-up to his nomination.

data.title: Armstrong World upgraded to Outperform at Evercore ISI after Q1 miss
data.description:
Armstrong World (AWI) down 0.9% in Wednesday's trading as Evercore ISI upgraded shares to Outperform from In-Line with a $200 price target, a day after the building products company posted Q1 adjusted earnings that missed analyst estimates.

Q1 profit fell to $66.8M, or $1.55/share, from $69.1M, or $1.58/share, in the year-earlier quarter, while revenues rose 7% Y/Y to a Q1 record $409.9M, including in-line sales in the Mineral Fiber and Architectural Specialties units, driven by both average unit value and volume growth, but with AS adjusted EBITDA margins falling short of expectations due to some non-recurring items.

Overall, Armstrong (AWI) posted solid top-line results, Evercore ISI analyst Stephen Kim said, even as management described the market as flattish in the quarter but noted it is starting to see signs of better discretionary demand; however, the company remains cautious given the uncertain macro and geopolitical backdrop.

However, Kim said he thinks Armstrong World (AWI) is well-positioned given its modest exposure to tariff and input costs, which it has been able to offset through pricing and productivity, and the company has a proven growth algorithm with strong AUV in Mineral Fiber, healthy expansion and scaling in Architectural Specialties, and innovation further supporting growth.

data.title: Meta to run some servers longer due to memory shortage - WSJ
data.description:
Meta Platforms (META) is extending the useful life of some of its data center servers from six to seven years due to a "significant server supply deficit" caused by the shortage of memory chips, reported The Wall Street Journal on Wednesday.

According to the article, citing two internal memos, Meta said the deficit affecting the company's non-AI servers is expected to last through 2027.

"We did not anticipate the hardware demand growth that we are seeing in the industry," the memo said, as per The Wall Street Journal. It added that "looking forward to 2027, the binding constraint includes critical server commodities -- particularly DRAM and HDDs."

Meta did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

With artificial intelligence rapidly growing, the memory chip business is seeing a boost. However, the prioritization of servers meant for AI systems and cloud service providers has led to shortages for other markets, including PCs, smartphones, as well as Meta's non-AI servers.

Meta servers' annualized failure rate is expected to go from 4.8% to 7.4% because of the lengthened service life, WSJ reported, citing the memo.

The article added that a separate memo said the changes "do not come without risks" and that projected failure rates at eight years are too high to justify extending the life of its servers that long.

Other companies, including Intel and Dell, are also facing a similar memory chip shortage, leading to higher costs for products.

data.title: Powell's Fed tenure ranks among the strongest eras for U.S. equities since 1970
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) has delivered a strong performance during Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s tenure, underscoring a resilient equity market despite a period marked by pandemic shocks, aggressive rate hikes, and persistent inflation concerns.

Since Powell assumed leadership in February 2018, the benchmark index has posted an annualized gain of 14.7%, ranking as the third-best performance among Fed chairs dating back to 1970.

Only the tenures of Paul Volcker and William Miller recorded higher annualized returns, though both periods were significantly shorter and occurred under very different macroeconomic conditions. Powell’s tenure stands out for balancing sustained market gains alongside one of the most rapid monetary tightening cycles in modern history.

The data highlights how equity markets have remained supported by strong corporate earnings, liquidity dynamics, and investor confidence, even as borrowing costs climbed. It also reinforces the broader historical trend of markets delivering positive returns across varying Federal Reserve leadership regimes. 

S&P 500 Funds: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Clearwater Paper plunges after quarterly losses widen; TD Cowen downgrades
data.description:
Clearwater Paper (CLW) down 14.5% in Wednesday's trading to as low as $11.73, its lowest intraday price since 2009, after posting a Q1 loss and a 5% Y/Y decline in revenue to $360M.

Q1 net loss increased to $13M, or $0.80/share, from a net loss of $6M, or $0.38/share, in the year-earlier quarter, and adjusted EBITDA from continuing operations fell to $2M from $30M in the same quarter last year, primarily due to lower market pricing and severe weather impacts.

TD Cowen downgraded shares to Hold from Buy with a $17 price target, cut from $19, saying Clearwater (CLW) has compelling long-term earnings potential and balance sheet flexibility, but analyst Sean Steuart said he is skeptical that the industry supply response so far is enough to support the company's H1 and 2026 SBS paperboard price increase initiatives.

Steuart said he expects downward revisions to Clearwater's (CLW) 2026-27 consensus estimates, reflecting a slower rate of price improvement and higher unit costs.

data.title: OGE plans Google data center contracts while reaffirming $2.43 2026 EPS guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: OGE Energy Corp. (OGE) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "This morning, we reported consolidated earnings of $0.24 per share" and "Even with milder weather in the first quarter, we remain confident in our 2026 guidance and in the foundation we are building for 2027 and beyond" (Chairman, President & CEO R. Trauschke).
  • "In the coming days, we will file long-term special contracts with Google to serve multiple previously announced data centers in Oklahoma with the Oklahoma Corporation Commission" and "Google will also pay 100% of the cost to connect to the grid and its fair share to power the data center sites" (Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke).
  • "We commissioned the 98-megawatt tinker power plant in February" and "expect 450 megawatts of new CTs at Horseshoe Lake to come online in the fourth quarter" while "also breaking ground on 2 additional 450-megawatt units" (Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke).
  • "So including the aforementioned capacity agreements, this 1.7 gigawatts of capacity strengthens our system today and positions us well for continued growth ahead" (Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke).
  • "We are finalizing a stand-alone large load tariff and expect to file it with the Oklahoma Corporation Commission no later than July 1" and "We continue to prepare for a rate review filing later this year with new rates anticipated in '27" (Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke).
  • "Consolidated net income was approximately $50 million or $0.24 per diluted share" and "We expect to achieve our consolidated earnings guidance of $2.43 per share with a range of $2.38 to $2.48, assuming normal weather for the balance of the year" (Chief Financial Officer Charles Walworth).

Outlook

  • "We expect to achieve our consolidated earnings guidance of $2.43 per share with a range of $2.38 to $2.48" and "We maintain our guidance for the year at 4% to 6%" for weather-normalized load growth (CFO Walworth).
  • "This announcement is consistent with what's in our IRP" and "this one by itself is not incremental" (CFO Walworth).
  • "We're not really going to get into that until those projects are rolled into the capital plan" and "those catalysts are out there to extend that expectation" in reference to the long-term EPS growth target discussion (CFO Walworth).

Financial results

  • "Consolidated net income was approximately $50 million or $0.24 per diluted share compared to $63 million or $0.31 per share in the same period of 2025" (CFO Walworth).
  • "In our core business, the electric company achieved net income of approximately $58 million or $0.28 per diluted share compared to $71 million or $0.35 per share" and "The decrease in net income was primarily driven by mild first quarter weather and the timing of O&M" (CFO Walworth).
  • "The holding company reported a loss of approximately $8 million or $0.04 per diluted share, consistent with the prior year" (CFO Walworth).
  • "In April, we completed a debt issuance at the electric utility, which satisfies our financing needs for 2026 under the current plan" and "We have flexibility between now and May 2027 to exercise the approximately 4.6 million shares in the forward equity agreements" (CFO Walworth).
  • "Moody's revised the outlooks for both OGE Energy and OG&E to stable from negative and affirmed all ratings" and "We continue to target credit supportive metrics and expect to maintain FFO to debt around 17% over the planning horizon" (CFO Walworth).

Q&A

  • Whitney Mutalemwa, Wells Fargo: "HB 2992... explicitly requires separate large load tariffs and cost causation protections"—does it improve negotiations? Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke: "I think it's clearly supportive of the direction we've been heading" and "Protecting the existing customer base has been paramount to us from day 1."
  • Mutalemwa, Wells Fargo: how to "sequence" rate filings and large-load investment? Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke: "We're not going to provide a full portfolio filing. We're going to file them as the negotiation is complete" and "our intention around the large load tariff is to actually protect those customers."
  • Michael Brown, Barclays: what is OG&E looking for in the large-load tariff and what upfront commitments? Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke: "we would fully expect any large load customer to pay" connection-related amounts "in advance" and the tariff should include "contract terms and security" and "pricing structures and charge allocations" to "protect the existing customer base."
  • Brown, Barclays: timing to communicate upside from Google/transmission/IRP and impact to EPS growth? CFO Walworth: "It's going to be just like the playbook that we did last year" and "as soon as that's buttoned up... we'll be prepared to layer that into our plan and discuss financing and then how that impacts earnings."
  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: was 5% to 7% long-term growth omitted? CFO Walworth: "we didn't mention that because it's unchanged in the near term" and "this really allows us to extend that runway."
  • Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: is Google load incremental and what is the cadence of additional conversations? CFO Walworth: "this one by itself is not incremental" and "I would characterize it as continuing and consistent."
  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg: Seminole-to-Shreveport in-service timing and right-of-way questions? CFO Walworth: "that's part of the process we're going through right now is firming that up" and the routing is "new" right-of-way.
  • Stephen D’Ambrisi, RBC: capacity contracts vs. owning generation assets? Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke: "It's our intent to own and operate these assets" and "we're the ones holding the ball... when the severe weather comes."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts slightly negative to neutral, pressing for clarity on tariffs, sequencing, load visibility, financing needs, and transmission timing, including "how can that kind of change your financing needs?" (Barclays) and "have you determined whether there's an additional equity need" (Ladenburg).
  • Management slightly positive and controlled, repeatedly emphasizing customer protections and a measured approach, including "we remain confident in our 2026 guidance" (Chairman, President & CEO Trauschke) and "guidance affirmed" (CFO Walworth).
  • Compared with last quarter, management shifted from "finalizing a 1 gigawatt contract... customer X" to naming "Google" and emphasizing filing timing, while keeping similar caution on when upside will be incorporated into the capital plan.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The large-load narrative advanced from "finalizing a 1 gigawatt contract" with "customer X" (Q4) to "Google is the customer previously referred to as customer X" with imminent contract filings and "minimum charges" and "exit provisions" highlighted (Q1).
  • The Oklahoma large-load tariff moved from a midyear expectation (Q4) to "no later than July 1" (Q1), alongside more explicit discussion of Oklahoma legislation support.
  • Financing messaging progressed from planning for utility debt and equity completed last year (Q4) to "debt issuance... satisfies our financing needs for 2026" and Moody’s outlook improving to "stable from negative" (Q1).

Risks and concerns

  • "mild first quarter weather" and "temporary outages at a few large customers" were cited as headwinds to results (CFO Walworth).
  • Stranded-cost and cost-allocation risk around large loads was addressed through "minimum charges and exit provisions" and "strong credit support" (CFO Walworth).
  • Execution and timing uncertainty remained around transmission and storage: "still work in progress" on the Seminole-to-Shreveport line details and "we... anticipate an order" in August for Frontier storage pre-approval (CFO Walworth).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as weather-impacted but maintained full-year EPS guidance of $2.43 (range $2.38 to $2.48), while elevating large-load visibility by identifying Google as the previously disclosed customer X and signaling imminent regulatory filings for long-term contracts and a stand-alone large-load tariff by July 1. The call emphasized customer-protection mechanics, near-term generation additions, and pending regulatory catalysts (Frontier storage and SPP transmission notices) that management said will be incorporated into an updated capital, financing, and earnings framework once approvals and orders are in hand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Majority of FOMC didn't see need to change 'easing bias' of Fed statement: Powell press conference
data.description:
"If we need to hike, we would certainly signal that, and we would do it," Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said during his post-decision press conference on Wednesday. Still, "nobody is calling for a hike right now," he added.

"Our commitment to (reducing inflation to 2% sustainably) is never-ending and unshakeable," he said.

The press conference comes after the Federal Open Market Committee again kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting.

Three dissents at the meeting favored a two-sided forward guidance instead of its mild “easing bias” in today's statement. 

"The center is moving to a more neutral place," Powell said. But the majority of the FOMC didn't feel it needed to signal that there's a potential for the next move to be a rate increase or decrease.

"Powell’s final press conference is effectively the official announcement of the end of the rate-cutting cycle," said Yimin Xu, on behalf of Seeking Alpha analyst Cestrian Capital Research. "The Fed is no longer concerned about the labor market and is firmly focusing on the impact of the energy shock alongside tariffs. Despite the huge uncertainties still ahead, the next move is most likely a hike, and the market is starting to price this in more aggressively."

Regarding the “easing bias” in the Fed statement, Powell said the FOMC is "working on the hypothesis" that tariffs would lead to a one-time price increase and fade over time. "We expect that to be happening in the next two quarters," he added. But the oil shock complicates the picture. "With energy, it's so hard to say," he said. Oil shocks tend to revert quickly. The policymakers want to see progress on inflation settling down before resuming rate cuts.

In explaining the reason for not removing the bias, he said, It's a form of forward guidance. "Markets are not confused about our reaction function," he added.

If the Iran conflict continues and that bleeds into inflation, then the Fed may have to increase rates rather than cut.

Developing… Check back for updates.
